does: This report presents gpt-oss-safeguard-120b and gpt-oss-safeguard-20b, open-weight reasoning models created through post-training of gpt-oss base models to perform policy-conditional content labeling. The models are specifically trained to reason from provided policies and apply them to classify content appropriately. The report documents the models' capabilities through systematic safety evaluations, comparing performance against the underlying gpt-oss baseline models. The work establishes these reasoning-augmented models as tools for content moderation that can adapt to different policy frameworks by incorporating policy descriptions into the reasoning process.
connects: This work builds on the open-weight gpt-oss model family, extending their capabilities toward safety-critical applications in content moderation. It responds to growing demand for interpretable, policy-grounded decision-making in large language models—a direction pursued by prior work on chain-of-thought reasoning and instruction-following. The approach of post-training specialized models for safety tasks follows established practices in reinforcement learning from human feedback and supervised fine-tuning for alignment. By emphasizing open weights and reasoning transparency, the work connects to broader efforts in interpretable AI and the democratization of capable safety tools, moving beyond proprietary content moderation systems.
enables: This release of open-weight safeguard models enables the research community to build customized content moderation systems adapted to specific policy frameworks and cultural contexts. Future work can leverage these models to explore policy-conditional reasoning at scale, studying how reasoning quality improves with model scale and how different policy formulations affect labeling consistency. The models provide a foundation for developing human-in-the-loop moderation tools, testing adversarial robustness of policy-based classifiers, and investigating generalization across policy domains. This infrastructure could support research into the interpretability of policy reasoning and the development of more nuanced safety evaluation benchmarks.
